title: Pearl Harbor: The Attack That Woke a Sleeping Giant
tagline: Two waves. Ninety minutes. A date that would live in infamy.

music.notes: Opening: calm Sunday-morning strings, almost peaceful, undercut with a single low ominous tone. First bombs: sudden percussive shock, brass stabs. Arizona's magazine explosion: the music drops out entirely for one beat, then returns devastating and low. Second wave: relentless, mechanical rhythm. Aftermath: mournful solo strings over silence. Roosevelt's speech: rising, resolute brass, the theme hardening into something unbreakable. Modern legacy/Black Tears: slow, elegiac, the theme returning one final time, quiet and heavy.

voice_style.pacing: calm and measured early, sudden and urgent through the attack, slow and weighted in the aftermath
voice_style.character: documentary narrator who treats December 7, 1941 as a morning that genuinely changed the world, without ever reaching for melodrama the facts don't need

youtube_title: Pearl Harbor: 90 Minutes That Changed the World | Gods & Glory EP6
youtube_description:
On the morning of December 7, 1941, the United States Pacific Fleet was at rest in Pearl Harbor. Within 90 minutes, over 2,400 Americans were dead, 21 ships were sunk or damaged, and the United States was at war.

This is the real story of Pearl Harbor — not the movie version. The actual timeline, the actual numbers, and the detail most people never learn: oil is still leaking from the USS Arizona today, more than 80 years later.

Gods & Glory — history's turning points, explained properly.

scenes.title: A Quiet Sunday Morning
scenes.narration: December 7th, 1941. Sunday morning in Pearl Harbor, Hawaii. Sailors were sleeping in, eating breakfast, getting ready for church. The Pacific Fleet sat at anchor in neat rows, exactly as it had for months. At 7:48 AM, that calm ended. A formation of aircraft appeared over the island, and the pilot leading them radioed back to his carrier four words that would open the deadliest surprise attack in American history: Tora, Tora, Tora. Tiger, Tiger, Tiger. Total surprise achieved.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Peaceful dawn over Pearl Harbor, battleships at anchor in Battleship Row, sailors on deck in the calm morning light. Then a sudden cut: Japanese Zero fighters and dive bombers breaking through clouds against a brightening sky, the first wave of the attack beginning. 16:9, photorealistic, dramatic morning light.

scenes.title: Why Japan Attacked
scenes.narration: This was not a random act of aggression. For years, Japan had been at war in China and expanding through Southeast Asia, and the United States had responded with economic pressure — culminating in an oil embargo that threatened to strangle the Japanese war machine within two years. Japan's military leadership concluded they had two choices: negotiate a withdrawal they considered humiliating, or strike first and seize the resources they needed before the embargo crippled them. Admiral Isoroku Yamamoto, who had studied at Harvard and traveled across America, personally doubted Japan could win a long war against US industrial capacity. He planned the attack anyway, hoping a knockout blow to the Pacific Fleet would buy Japan six months to a year of free conquest before America recovered.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Japanese naval command room, officers around a strategic map of the Pacific, tension and calculation. Admiral Yamamoto depicted in profile, contemplative. Cut to oil tankers and embargo imagery, the economic pressure made visual. 16:9, muted tones, serious tone.

scenes.title: The Fleet That Was Sitting There
scenes.narration: The US Pacific Fleet's home base at Pearl Harbor held the battleships that, in 1941, were still considered the measure of naval power. Eight of them sat in Battleship Row that morning: Arizona, Oklahoma, West Virginia, California, Nevada, Tennessee, Maryland, and Pennsylvania. Most of the fleet's sailors had no reason to expect an attack — Pearl Harbor was thousands of miles from Japan, and American intelligence believed any Japanese strike, if it came, would hit closer to home, in the Philippines or Southeast Asia. That assumption was wrong, and it was about to get a lot of people killed.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Wide aerial view of Battleship Row at Pearl Harbor, the massive grey battleships lined up at anchor, peaceful Sunday morning activity on deck. 16:9, documentary realism, calm before the storm.

scenes.title: The Radar Warning Nobody Believed
scenes.narration: At 7:02 AM, an Army radar station on Oahu picked up a large formation of aircraft approaching from the north. The two privates operating the station radioed it in. The officer who took the call, a lieutenant named Kermit Tyler, assumed it was a flight of American B-17 bombers expected that morning and told them, famously, 'don't worry about it.' Forty-six minutes later, the first bombs fell. It remains one of the most studied missed warnings in military history — not because anyone was incompetent, but because nobody in the chain of command was prepared to imagine what was actually happening.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Army radar station interior, two young soldiers at a radar scope watching a cluster of blips, one picking up a phone with a concerned expression. Tense, dim lighting, dramatic irony. 16:9.

scenes.title: 7:48 AM — The First Wave
scenes.narration: 183 Japanese aircraft, launched from six carriers sitting undetected 230 miles north of Oahu, struck in the first wave: dive bombers, torpedo bombers, and fighters. They hit the airfields first, destroying American planes before they could even take off — most US aircraft were parked wingtip to wingtip, an anti-sabotage precaution that made them perfect targets for strafing runs. Simultaneously, torpedo bombers swept in low over the harbor, releasing torpedoes specially modified with wooden fins to work in Pearl Harbor's shallow water. Within minutes, Battleship Row was burning.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Japanese torpedo bombers flying low over Pearl Harbor's water, torpedoes released, explosions beginning along Battleship Row. American aircraft burning on the ground at Hickam Field. Chaos and smoke. 16:9, intense action.

scenes.title: The USS Arizona
scenes.narration: At 8:06 AM, a Japanese armor-piercing bomb struck the USS Arizona near her forward ammunition magazine. The resulting explosion was catastrophic — it tore the bow of the ship apart and killed nearly half of everyone who died at Pearl Harbor that day in a single instant. Of the 1,177 sailors and Marines who died aboard the Arizona, many were never recovered. The ship sank in nine minutes.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. USS Arizona engulfed in a massive explosion, the forward section erupting in flame and black smoke, debris and water thrown skyward. Devastating, somber, historically accurate. 16:9.

scenes.title: The USS Oklahoma Capsizes
scenes.narration: The USS Oklahoma took multiple torpedo hits in the opening minutes and began rolling over almost immediately. Within twelve minutes she had capsized completely, trapping hundreds of men inside the hull below the waterline. For days afterward, rescue crews could hear the trapped men tapping on the steel from inside the overturned ship. Rescuers worked frantically, cutting through the hull with torches, and managed to save 32 men. Four hundred and twenty-nine sailors and Marines on the Oklahoma did not make it out.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. USS Oklahoma rolling over in the harbor, men jumping into burning oil-slicked water, rescue boats approaching. Desperate, chaotic, the human scale of the disaster. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Second Wave
scenes.narration: At 8:54 AM, a second wave of 171 Japanese aircraft arrived, targeting ships that had survived the first strike and continuing the assault on the airfields. American resistance had organized in the chaos — sailors manned anti-aircraft guns, some still in their pajamas, and shot down several attacking planes. But the second wave inflicted further damage across the harbor before the Japanese aircraft withdrew around 9:45 AM. In under two hours, the attack was over.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. American sailors manning anti-aircraft guns on burning ship decks, firing into a sky full of Japanese planes, smoke and chaos everywhere. Determined resistance amid disaster. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Numbers
scenes.narration: When the smoke cleared: 2,403 Americans dead, 1,178 wounded. Twenty-one ships sunk or damaged. One hundred eighty-eight aircraft destroyed. On the Japanese side: 64 dead, 29 aircraft lost. It was, by any measure, a devastating tactical victory for Japan and one of the worst single days in American military history.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Somber aerial view of the harbor after the attack, multiple ships burning and listing, oil fires across the water, smoke columns rising into the sky. Documentary gravitas. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Mistake Japan Made
scenes.narration: Here is the detail that decided the rest of the Pacific War. All three of the Pacific Fleet's aircraft carriers — the ships that would actually win the war that followed — were out at sea on maneuvers and missed the attack entirely. Japanese commander Admiral Nagumo also chose not to launch a third wave to destroy Pearl Harbor's fuel depots, repair yards, and submarine base, judging the risk to his own carriers too high. Those facilities turned out to matter more than the battleships. Because they survived intact, the US Navy could repair and refuel ships immediately and kept fighting from Pearl Harbor for the rest of the war.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. US aircraft carrier at sea, untouched and powerful, juxtaposed against the burning harbor in the distance. The strategic irony made visual — the real weapon survived. 16:9.

scenes.title: Doris Miller
scenes.narration: Doris Miller was a mess attendant on the USS West Virginia — Black sailors in the segregated 1941 Navy were barred from combat roles and confined to jobs like cooking and laundry. When the attack began, Miller helped move wounded sailors to safety, then manned an anti-aircraft gun he had never been trained to use and fired on the attacking Japanese planes until he ran out of ammunition. He became the first Black American to receive the Navy Cross, the Navy's third-highest honor. He died two years later when his ship was torpedoed in the Pacific. The Navy commissioned an aircraft carrier in his name in 2020 — the first carrier ever named for a Black American and an enlisted sailor.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Doris Miller manning an anti-aircraft gun on the burning deck of the USS West Virginia, determined expression, smoke and chaos around him. Heroic, dignified, historically grounded. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Men Trapped Inside
scenes.narration: Not every story from that morning ended in rescue. Inside the capsized Oklahoma and the wrecked Arizona, men who survived the initial explosions were trapped in pockets of air with no way out. Some left messages scratched into the metal. Rescue crews worked for days, but for many trapped below the waterline, the air simply ran out before anyone could reach them. It is one of the grimmest, least-told parts of December 7th — that for some sailors, death wasn't instant. It came slowly, in the dark, waiting.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Somber, restrained depiction: the dark interior of an overturned ship hull, a faint shaft of light, the suggestion of trapped sailors without graphic detail. Respectful, heavy tone. 16:9.

scenes.title: December 8th — A Date Which Will Live in Infamy
scenes.narration: The next day, President Franklin Roosevelt addressed a joint session of Congress. His speech lasted just over seven minutes. The line everyone remembers is the opening: 'Yesterday, December 7th, 1941 — a date which will live in infamy — the United States of America was suddenly and deliberately attacked.' Congress declared war on Japan within an hour, with only one dissenting vote. Three days later, Germany and Italy declared war on the United States, and a conflict that had been raging in Europe and Asia for years became, for America, one single global war.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. President Roosevelt addressing a packed, solemn joint session of Congress, dramatic lighting, the weight of the moment. Newspaper headlines declaring war flashing past. 16:9, historic gravitas.

scenes.title: The Sleeping Giant
scenes.narration: Admiral Yamamoto is often quoted as saying the attack had merely 'awakened a sleeping giant and filled him with a terrible resolve' — though historians have never confirmed he actually said it; the line first appeared in print decades later. Whether or not Yamamoto said those exact words, he understood the danger of what he'd done. Pearl Harbor didn't cripple America's ability to fight. It united a divided country that had spent two years arguing over whether to enter the war at all, and overnight ended that argument completely.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. American shipyards and factories shifting into wartime production, workers on assembly lines building ships and planes at massive scale. The industrial awakening made visual. 16:9.

scenes.title: What Came Next
scenes.narration: Within six months, the US Navy's surviving carriers won a decisive victory at Midway, sinking four Japanese fleet carriers and breaking Japan's naval initiative in the Pacific for good. American industrial capacity, which Yamamoto had feared all along, came online at a scale Japan could never match — by the end of the war, the US had built more aircraft carriers than Japan had built warships of any kind. Yamamoto's six-month window of free conquest had barely closed before the tide began turning against Japan permanently.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Montage of American naval and industrial buildup: shipyards launching new carriers, the Battle of Midway depicted with carrier aircraft, the scale of American war production. Triumphant but measured tone. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Survivors Who Came Home
scenes.narration: Many sailors who survived Pearl Harbor went on to fight throughout the rest of the war. Some, like Doris Miller, did not survive to see it end. Others lived for decades afterward, and for the rest of their lives carried December 7th as the defining morning of their existence. Survivors' associations met annually at Pearl Harbor for reunions well into the 2010s, their numbers shrinking every year, until the very last verified USS Arizona survivors died in their late nineties and early hundreds in the years just before this episode was made.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Elderly Pearl Harbor survivors in dress uniform at a memorial reunion, saluting, the contrast between the young sailors of 1941 and the old men decades later. Respectful, elegiac. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Black Tears of the Arizona
scenes.narration: Here is the detail almost nobody learns in school. The USS Arizona was never raised. It still rests on the harbor floor exactly where it sank, and more than eighty years later, it is still leaking oil — roughly a quart a day, drop by drop, rising to the surface in a visible sheen that visitors to the memorial can see from above. Divers estimate as much as five hundred thousand gallons of fuel oil remain trapped inside the wreck. The Navy has deliberately chosen not to remove it: pumping it out risks structural collapse and a much larger environmental release, and the ship is also a designated war grave. Hawaiians call the slow seep of oil the Black Tears of the Arizona — the ship, they say, still mourning the men inside it.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Aerial view of the USS Arizona Memorial, the visible oil sheen rising and spreading on the water's surface above the sunken wreck below, somber and beautiful. Elegiac, contemplative. 16:9.

scenes.title: Buried at Sea, Decades Later
scenes.narration: Since 1982, the Navy has allowed surviving crew members of the USS Arizona to choose, upon their deaths, to be interred inside the ship alongside the shipmates they lost in 1941. Navy divers carry an urn of cremated remains down to the wreck and place it inside one of the ship's gun turrets, reuniting each survivor with the crew they served beside for one morning in December, more than half a century before. More than forty men have chosen this. Roughly nine hundred of the original crew remain inside the ship to this day, never recovered, the wreck itself serving as their grave.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Navy divers in the dark water beside the submerged USS Arizona wreck, carefully placing an urn into the ship's structure, solemn underwater lighting filtering down from above. Reverent, quiet. 16:9.

scenes.title: Was It Really a Surprise?
scenes.narration: For decades, a conspiracy theory has circulated claiming Roosevelt knew about the attack in advance and let it happen to justify entering the war. Declassified records and the consensus of professional historians do not support this. American codebreakers had cracked Japanese diplomatic codes and knew war was likely coming soon, but the specific target — Pearl Harbor itself — was not known in advance, and the failure was one of analysis and imagination, not a deliberate conspiracy. The honest version is less satisfying than the conspiracy: it was a colossal intelligence failure, not a secret plan.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Codebreakers in a dim intelligence office working over intercepted Japanese diplomatic cables, papers and decryption equipment, tense focused atmosphere. Historically grounded, not sensationalized. 16:9.

scenes.title: What Pearl Harbor Actually Was
scenes.narration: Pearl Harbor was a tactical masterpiece and a strategic catastrophe — for Japan. It achieved near-total surprise and devastated the battleships America had built its prewar naval doctrine around. But it missed the carriers, missed the fuel and repair infrastructure, and it guaranteed a war against an opponent with vastly greater industrial capacity, fighting on terms that opponent would ultimately dictate. Most attacks that look brilliant in the first ninety minutes are judged on what happens in the years that follow. By that standard, Pearl Harbor failed completely.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Split visual: the burning harbor in 1941 contrasted with the overwhelming scale of American wartime industrial production that followed. The strategic verdict made visual. 16:9.

scenes.title: Why This Still Matters
scenes.narration: Pearl Harbor is taught as the day America entered World War Two, but its deeper lesson is about the danger of assuming an enemy will act the way you expect them to. American commanders expected a different kind of attack, in a different place, and that assumption — not a lack of courage or resources — is what let the Japanese achieve total surprise. Military and intelligence services still study Pearl Harbor today as the textbook case of a failure of imagination, not a failure of information. The warning signs existed. Nobody connected them in time.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Modern military analysts and historians studying Pearl Harbor maps and documents, the lesson translated into a contemporary intelligence-briefing setting. Thoughtful, analytical tone. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Memorial Today
scenes.narration: Today, the USS Arizona Memorial sits directly above the wreck, a white structure spanning the sunken hull without touching it. Over a million visitors come each year. Names of the dead are inscribed on a marble wall. Below the water, the ship that once carried over a thousand men into battle still holds most of them, and still bleeds its slow black tears into the harbor it never left.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. The white USS Arizona Memorial structure spanning the water above the wreck, visitors walking through respectfully, the marble wall of names. Serene, dignified, present-day. 16:9.

scenes.title: What Happened — The Summary
scenes.narration: December 7th, 1941. Japan launches a surprise carrier strike on the US Pacific Fleet at Pearl Harbor. Two waves, ninety minutes, 2,403 Americans dead. The battleships are devastated — but the carriers, the fuel depots, and the repair yards survive untouched. The next day, America declares war. Within six months, the US Navy breaks Japanese naval power at Midway. The USS Arizona is never raised — it remains on the harbor floor today, still leaking oil, still holding most of its crew. A sleeping giant, woken with a terrible resolve.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Summary title card sequence: key moments in bold text over historical imagery — the attack, Roosevelt's speech, Midway, the memorial today. Then a final slow shot of the Arizona Memorial at sunset. 16:9.

scenes.title: Japan's Rise — From Isolation to Pacific Power
scenes.narration: Japan in 1853 was a feudal society that had been closed to the outside world for over two centuries. In 1853, American Commodore Matthew Perry arrived with steam-powered warships and demanded Japan open its ports to trade. Japan complied, humiliated. What followed was the Meiji Restoration of 1868: Japan's ruling class decided to modernize at unprecedented speed rather than be colonized like China. They studied Western industrial, military, and governmental systems systematically, adopting what worked and discarding what didn't. By 1894, Japan defeated China in a war. By 1905, Japan defeated Russia — a European power — in the Russo-Japanese War, shocking the entire world. By 1914, Japan was fighting alongside the Allies in World War I as an equal partner. In forty years, Japan had gone from feudal isolation to acknowledged great power. The problem was what came next: an industrialized Japan with imperial ambitions, a growing military class that modeled itself on imperial Germany, and a region — Asia — where Western colonial powers were declining but not gone.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Japan in 1853: feudal, isolated, samurai culture intact. Perry's black ships arriving — steam-powered, enormous, utterly foreign. Japan's shock and the Meiji Restoration decision. The speed of modernization: factories, railways, universities, a modern army and navy built in two decades. 1894: Japan defeating China in the First Sino-Japanese War. 1905: the Battle of Tsushima — Japan destroying the Russian fleet, shocking Europe. Japan at the Paris Peace Conference 1919 — equal power at the table. Then: the military faction's rise in the 1930s, the decision that Japan needs an empire, the pivot from modernization to expansion. 40 years from feudal isolation to Pacific empire. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Oil Embargo — The Economic Weapon That Made War Inevitable
scenes.narration: The specific trigger for Pearl Harbor was an oil embargo. Japan had been fighting in China since 1937, committing atrocities that drew international condemnation including the Rape of Nanjing. In July 1941, Japan occupied French Indochina. The United States, Britain, and the Netherlands responded with a full oil embargo. Japan imported eighty percent of its oil from the United States. At current consumption rates — especially military consumption — Japan had roughly eighteen months of oil reserves. The choice was stark: withdraw from China and Indochina, losing the empire Japan's military had spent a decade building; or secure the oil fields of the Dutch East Indies — modern Indonesia — by force. Securing the Dutch East Indies meant war with the Netherlands, Britain, and almost certainly the United States. The planners in Tokyo calculated that if war with America was coming anyway, it was better to begin it on Japanese terms, with a decisive first strike that would buy time to consolidate the Southeast Asian empire before America could mobilize. Yamamoto's plan was the operational solution to a strategic box Japan had walked itself into.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. The economic chokepoint: Japan's oil supply shown as a flow chart — 80% from the United States. The embargo announcement: FDR signing the executive order, the freeze of Japanese assets. Japan's oil reserves gauge dropping: 18 months at current consumption. The strategic calculation in Tokyo: map showing the Dutch East Indies oil fields, the line connecting them to Japan's war machine. The binary choice: surrender the empire or seize the oil. Japanese military leadership calculating that war with America is already happening — the question is only when and how. The logic of Pearl Harbor: not aggression for its own sake but a desperate calculation that a first strike was better than waiting. 16:9.

scenes.title: Yamamoto — The Admiral Who Knew He Was Starting Something He Couldn't Finish
scenes.narration: Isoroku Yamamoto is one of history's stranger figures: the architect of Pearl Harbor who believed the attack was a catastrophic mistake. Yamamoto had lived in the United States, studied at Harvard, served as a naval attaché in Washington. He knew American industrial capacity firsthand. When his superiors asked him to plan a strike against the US Pacific Fleet, he complied professionally, designed the best plan possible — and then told them it would not work. 'I can run wild for six months to a year,' he said, 'but after that I have no expectation of success.' His 'sleeping giant' quote — 'I fear all we have done is to awaken a sleeping giant and fill him with a terrible resolve' — may or may not have been spoken at the moment, but it accurately describes his documented assessment before the attack. He planned Pearl Harbor with professional brilliance and executed it with near-perfection, knowing throughout that the best possible outcome was buying time for a war he did not believe Japan could win.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Yamamoto: his biography — Harvard University photograph, his years in Washington as a naval attaché, his poker games with American officers. His documented opposition to war with America — letters and statements shown. The order to plan Pearl Harbor arriving regardless. His professional execution: the training program at Kagoshima Bay, the torpedo modifications for shallow water, the coordination of six carriers. His documented statements about the outcome: 'I can run wild for six months.' The contrast: the architect of the most successful surprise attack in naval history, fully believing it would ultimately fail. His death in 1943: shot down on an inspection tour after American codebreakers decoded his itinerary. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Kido Butai — Six Carriers, Perfect Coordination
scenes.narration: The strike force that attacked Pearl Harbor — the Kido Butai, or Mobile Force — was the most powerful concentration of naval aviation ever assembled to that point. Six fleet carriers: Akagi, Kaga, Soryu, Hiryu, Shokaku, Zuikaku. Four hundred and eight aircraft. Two battleships, two heavy cruisers, eleven destroyers, and eight tankers. The tankers were essential: the route chosen went north through the empty Pacific, away from shipping lanes, refueling at sea. The force sailed from Hitokappu Bay in the Kurils on November 26, 1941 under strict radio silence, covering over three thousand miles in thirteen days without being detected. The Kido Butai was essentially the Japanese navy's entire offensive naval aviation capability committed to a single strike. If it had been detected and attacked, Japan would have lost the war in December 1941 rather than August 1945. The gamble was total.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. The Kido Butai in formation: six fleet carriers in two columns, flanked by battleships and cruisers. The route map: from Hitokappu Bay in the northern Kurils, the great northern arc across the empty Pacific, staying far from shipping lanes. The radio silence: every radio transmitter locked and sealed. The at-sea refueling shown: tankers alongside carriers in heavy Pacific swells. The six carriers with their aircraft complements: 408 aircraft total shown as silhouettes. The fleet arriving at the launch point north of Hawaii, undetected. The scale of the gamble: Japan's entire offensive naval aviation capability in one place. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Intelligence Failures — All the Warning Signs That Were Missed
scenes.narration: The attack on Pearl Harbor was not completely without warning. In the days and weeks before December 7, American intelligence had multiple indicators that something significant was coming. The fourteen-part message from Tokyo to the Japanese embassy — decoded by American cryptographers the night of December 6 — indicated negotiations had been terminated, which historically precedes attack. The 'winds' code, a prearranged signal in Japanese weather broadcasts indicating war, had been established. A US submarine had sunk a midget submarine attempting to enter Pearl Harbor the night before. The Army radar operators at Opana Point detected the incoming aircraft at 7:02 AM and reported it — a duty officer told them it was probably American B-17s from the mainland. The Japanese consul in Honolulu had been sending detailed observations of the fleet's anchorage pattern. Every one of these signals was either misinterpreted, stuck in bureaucratic routing, or simply not believed. The failure was not a lack of intelligence. It was a failure of imagination — the inability to believe that Japan would actually strike American territory on American soil.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. The intelligence failures laid out as a timeline: each warning signal appearing, each one ignored or misinterpreted. The 14-part message being decoded in Washington — shown to senior officials, not acted on urgently enough. The radar contact at Opana Point at 7:02 AM: the two operators' report, the duty officer's dismissal. The midget submarine sunk the night before — the report not transmitted urgently. The consul's shipping reports in the American intelligence system. Each warning shown as a lit signal, each one going dark as it was ignored. The structural failure: too many indicators for the bureaucracy to process, and the assumption underlying all of it — America would not be attacked on its own soil — making every warning seem less credible. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Attack — Wave by Wave
scenes.narration: At 7:48 AM on December 7, 1941, Commander Mitsuo Fuchida radioed the attack signal: 'Tora! Tora! Tora!' — Tiger, Tiger, Tiger — signaling complete surprise. The first wave of 183 aircraft arrived over Pearl Harbor in two groups: horizontal bombers targeting battleship row, torpedo bombers approaching at wave height, dive bombers targeting airfields. The torpedo attack was the most technically challenging: Pearl Harbor's average depth was only twelve meters, requiring specially modified torpedoes with wooden fins to prevent them from diving too deep. Seven of the eight battleships were hit. The USS Arizona exploded at 8:10 AM when a bomb penetrated to its forward magazine — 1,177 men died in the explosion, more than half of all American deaths in the attack. The USS Oklahoma capsized. The USS Nevada, the only battleship to get underway, was driven into shallow water to prevent her from sinking and blocking the harbor channel. The second wave arrived at 8:54 AM. By 10:00 AM it was over. Total American dead: 2,403. American aircraft destroyed: 188.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. The attack sequence: the two waves shown on a tactical map. The torpedo bombers at wave height, modified torpedoes skimming Pearl Harbor's shallow water. The first explosions on Battleship Row: Ford Island, the row of battleships hit in sequence. The Arizona: the penetrating bomb, the magazine detonation, the ship breaking apart and sinking in minutes, 1,177 dead. The Oklahoma capsizing. The Nevada making her run for the harbor entrance, driven aground. The second wave arriving. Anti-aircraft fire beginning — American gunners fighting back. The attack as a professional military operation: the extraordinary coordination of 408 aircraft across two waves. 10:00 AM: the skies clear. The damage assessment beginning. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Arizona Memorial — Where 1,177 Men Still Rest
scenes.narration: The USS Arizona still lies at the bottom of Pearl Harbor. It was never salvaged — the cost and difficulty of raising it were considered too great, and there was a deeper reason: more than nine hundred of the 1,177 men who died when she exploded are still entombed inside the hull. The ship is their grave. A white memorial structure was built spanning her sunken hull in 1962, designed by Alfred Preis, a Viennese-born architect who had himself been interned as an enemy alien in Hawaii during the war. A small amount of oil still seeps from the Arizona's fuel tanks every day — roughly a quart a day — creating small iridescent patches on the harbor surface. Visitors call it 'the tears of the Arizona.' This will continue, by engineering estimates, for another fifty to eighty years. Survivors of the attack have the option to be interred inside the hull after death, joining their shipmates. Thirty-eight chose this. The Arizona is simultaneously a war grave, a memorial, a museum, and a slowly weeping ruin. It is the most visited site in Hawaii.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. The USS Arizona underwater: the sunken hull visible through clear Hawaiian water, coral growing over decades. The oil seeping from the fuel tanks daily — small rainbow-colored patches on the surface. The white memorial structure spanning the hull above the waterline: Alfred Preis's design, its three sections representing the before, during, and after of the attack. The wall of names: all 1,177 dead inscribed. The survivors who chose to be interred in the hull — their urns placed inside. The visitors: thousands per day, standing above 900 men still entombed below. The oil droplets rising to the surface. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Decision Not to Launch a Third Strike — The Debate That Still Continues
scenes.narration: Fuchida and Genda, the planners of the Pearl Harbor attack, later argued that Vice Admiral Nagumo, commander of the Kido Butai, made the decisive mistake of the Pacific War by ordering the fleet to turn around rather than launch a third strike. A third strike could have targeted: the repair facilities, the dry docks, the fuel storage tanks — the industrial infrastructure of Pearl Harbor that the first two waves had largely left untouched. The fuel tanks alone held 4.5 million barrels of oil, the entire Pacific Fleet's supply. If they had been destroyed, the Pacific Fleet could not have operated from Pearl Harbor for months, possibly years. Nagumo's decision to withdraw was based on: uncertainty about the carriers' location, concern about a counterattack, and the professional judgment that the mission objectives had been achieved. He was not wrong that the mission had succeeded. The historical argument is that he left the most valuable targets intact, and the Pacific War was won, in part, from the fuel that was still in those tanks. The debate between 'enough' and 'more' defines much of Pearl Harbor scholarship.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. The debate visualized: Nagumo on the Akagi's bridge, his advisors presenting the case for and against a third strike. The targets that weren't hit: the repair facilities shown intact, the fuel tanks (4.5 million barrels) shown full and untouched. What a third strike could have destroyed. The carriers' unknown location — Nagumo's concern about being caught while rearming aircraft. The professional calculation: objectives met, risk increasing. The decision to withdraw. Then: the fuel tanks that stayed intact powering the American counteroffensive from Pearl Harbor to Midway to Tokyo Bay. Fuchida and Genda's post-war testimony. The argument from 'enough' to 'almost enough.' 16:9.

scenes.title: The Arsenal of Democracy — America's Industrial Response
scenes.narration: The Japanese strategic calculation — that America could be deterred by a decisive blow — failed to account for American industrial capacity in the way Yamamoto himself had warned. In the first year after Pearl Harbor, the United States built more warships than the entire Japanese navy possessed. The second year, it built more again. The automobile factories of Detroit converted to tanks and aircraft within months. Ford built a B-24 bomber every hour at the Willow Run plant by 1944. Women entered the workforce in numbers never seen before — Rosie the Riveter was a real demographic phenomenon, not just propaganda. Between 1941 and 1945, American industry produced 300,000 aircraft, 90,000 tanks, 71,000 ships, 15 million rifles. The GDP of the United States grew by 75% during the war years. Japan produced 60,000 aircraft in the same period and ran out of trained pilots. The attack on Pearl Harbor succeeded tactically and failed strategically because it activated the largest industrial mobilization in human history.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. American industrial mobilization: the transformation of Detroit. Assembly lines shown converting from automobiles to tanks, artillery, aircraft. The Willow Run B-24 plant: a bomber rolling off the line every hour by 1944. Women workers flooding factories — the actual demographic shift. Statistical graphics: US production 300,000 aircraft vs. Japan's 60,000. Ship production comparison. GDP growth chart. The contrast: Japan's war economy, designed for a short decisive conflict, versus the American industrial economy, designed to outlast anything. The sleeping giant awake: not an army of 7 million trained soldiers but an economy of 130 million people converting their entire productive capacity to war. 16:9.

scenes.title: Pearl Harbor in American Memory — The Attack That Never Left
scenes.narration: Pearl Harbor entered American consciousness in a way that no other military event had before it. FDR's Day of Infamy speech was heard by approximately ninety million Americans — over two-thirds of the population — within twenty-four hours. The phrase 'a date which will live in infamy' became one of the most quoted sentences in American history. For a generation, 'Remember Pearl Harbor' served the same function 'Remember the Maine' had in 1898 and 'Remember 9/11' would serve six decades later: a shorthand for why America was fighting and why sacrifice was necessary. The parallels between Pearl Harbor and September 11, 2001 are structural: a surprise attack on American soil by an enemy America had underestimated, followed by immediate total mobilization, followed by wars of response that extended far beyond their original scope. Pearl Harbor is not just a battle. It is the template through which Americans have understood every surprise attack since. The date is still observed. The survivors still gather. The oil still seeps from the Arizona.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Pearl Harbor in American memory: FDR's Day of Infamy speech, the radio broadcast reaching 90 million listeners. Recruitment lines the next day: thousands of young men signing up simultaneously. The phrase 'Remember Pearl Harbor' on posters, in songs, in speeches. The survivors: elderly veterans at annual memorial ceremonies. The structural comparison to 9/11: the same elements — surprise attack on American soil, total mobilization, wars of response. The Arizona memorial on December 7 each year: veterans, their children, their grandchildren, gathering above the sunken ship. The oil still coming up. Memory and monument and the slow weeping of a ship that never came home. 16:9.

scenes.title: FDR's Dilemma — How America Got Into a War It Didn't Want
scenes.narration: By late 1941, Franklin Roosevelt believed American entry into the war against Germany was both necessary and inevitable, but two-thirds of the American public opposed involvement. The America First movement — supported by Charles Lindbergh and thousands of politicians — was powerful enough to constrain his options. He had provided Britain with destroyers and lend-lease supplies, but each step toward involvement was politically costly. The Atlantic was already a shooting war — American destroyers had exchanged fire with German submarines — but undeclared. Pearl Harbor solved Roosevelt's political problem instantly. The question of American involvement, which had paralyzed politics for two years, was answered on the morning of December 7. Roosevelt went to Congress the next day for a declaration of war and received it with one dissenting vote.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. FDR in the White House, 1941: the maps of Europe and the Pacific. The isolationist pressure: America First rallies, Lindbergh speaking. The measures already taken: destroyers to Britain, Lend-Lease. The undeclared naval war with Germany. FDR's political calculation: wanting to enter the war, unable to politically justify it. Then: the morning of December 7. The phone call from the Navy. FDR's face. The problem solved by an enemy's action. The Congress the next day: the vote. The political paralysis of two years ended in eight minutes of Japanese bombing. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Code Breakers — What American Intelligence Actually Knew
scenes.narration: American intelligence in late 1941 had broken the Japanese diplomatic code — MAGIC — and was reading encrypted Japanese messages in near-real time. Intelligence analysts knew by late November that Japan was about to strike somewhere in the Pacific. The 'war warning' sent to Pacific commands on November 27 was accurate in its assessment of Japanese intent and wrong in its prediction of location. The intelligence strongly indicated an attack on Malaya, the Philippines, or the Dutch East Indies. Hawaii was considered unlikely — too far from Japan, too strongly defended. The failure was not the absence of intelligence but the inability to correctly interpret what the intelligence was saying. An attack on Pearl Harbor was inside the evidence. It was outside the analytical framework.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. American code breakers at work: the MAGIC operation, Japanese diplomatic traffic decoded. The late November intercepts: Japan about to move. The war warning of November 27. The analytical failure: the map showing where intelligence expected the attack — Malaya, Philippines, Dutch East Indies. Pearl Harbor not on the primary list. The intercepts that pointed to Hawaii: the Honolulu harbor depth soundings Japan requested — the British warning that Pearl Harbor was the target, dismissed. The gap between the intelligence and the analysis. Not the failure to gather information but the failure to believe what the information was saying. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Arizona — One Ship's Story
scenes.narration: At 8:06 AM on December 7, an armor-piercing bomb penetrated the Arizona's forward deck and detonated her forward ammunition magazine — approximately one million pounds of gunpowder. The explosion was instantaneous and total: the ship's bow lifted out of the water. Of the 1,177 men aboard the Arizona, 1,102 died. The entire ship's band died together — they had been on deck when the attack began. The Arizona sank in nine minutes. She still lies on the harbor bottom today, along with the remains of the 900 men who have never been recovered and whose families requested they remain entombed with their ship. The Arizona still leaks approximately one quart of oil per day from her fuel tanks — 'black tears,' as visitors call them — visible on the surface of the harbor. She has been leaking continuously since December 7, 1941.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. The USS Arizona at 8:06 AM: the armor-piercing bomb falling. The explosion: the forward magazine detonating, one million pounds of gunpowder, the bow lifting from the water. The Arizona's band — the entire ship's band, on deck together, killed together. The ship sinking in nine minutes. The harbor floor where she rests: the white memorial above her. The 900 men who remain entombed below. The oil still rising from her tanks after 80 years — the 'black tears.' Visitors watching the oil surface. A ship that is simultaneously a grave, a monument, and a living reminder. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Survivors' Stories — What It Was Like to Be There
scenes.narration: Pearl Harbor survivor testimony describes an experience that defied rational comprehension in the moment. Men who were eating breakfast heard explosions and assumed it was a training exercise. Men on deck saw Japanese aircraft with rising sun markings and couldn't initially process what they were seeing. Sailors below decks in sinking ships clawed through flooded passages in darkness. The oil covering the harbor surface caught fire, and swimmers navigated between burning patches. One of the most consistent elements in survivor testimony is time distortion: the attack lasted approximately ninety minutes, but survivors typically recall it as either much shorter or much longer. Another consistent element: virtually every survivor describes some moment where chance — turning left instead of right, happening to be away from their normal station — made the difference between living and dying.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Pearl Harbor survivor testimony: the faces of the men who were there. The initial moments — men at breakfast, the explosions starting. A sailor on deck seeing a Japanese plane with the rising sun, unable to initially comprehend it. Below decks on a sinking ship: darkness, water rising, the passage to a hatch. The oil-covered harbor surface on fire. The time distortion: survivors recounting 90 minutes as 15 minutes or 3 hours. The chance factor: the sailor who happened to be in the head instead of on deck, alive. The extraordinary randomness of survival overlaid on the randomness of death. 16:9.

scenes.title: Japan's Strategic Mistake — Why the Attack Doomed Japan
scenes.narration: Yamamoto, who planned the attack on Pearl Harbor, reportedly told his superiors before it: 'I can run wild for six months... after that, I have no expectation of success.' He was right about the timeline and right about the outcome. The attack achieved its tactical objectives — it delayed American naval operations for months. It failed strategically for three reasons: the American aircraft carriers were not in harbor and were undamaged; the oil storage tanks and repair facilities at Pearl Harbor were not destroyed; and the attack on American territory on a Sunday morning, without a declaration of war, produced a unifying rage in the American public that no amount of Japanese military success could subsequently dilute. Yamamoto understood this before he ordered the attack. He ordered it anyway because the alternative — accepting American economic pressure — was politically impossible in Tokyo.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Yamamoto's prediction: 'I can run wild for six months.' The tactical success: American battleships sinking, the Pacific fleet delayed. Then the three strategic failures: the aircraft carriers absent, safe. The oil storage and repair facilities undamaged — the harbor operational within days. The American public's reaction: the rage, the recruiting lines, the industrial mobilization. The factories converting to war production. The industrial comparison: American vs. Japanese production capacity. Yamamoto's grim arithmetic: Japan had started a war it could not win against an enemy it had made implacable. Tactical genius in the service of strategic disaster. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Midway Connection — How Pearl Harbor Was Avenged Six Months Later
scenes.narration: The Battle of Midway, fought June 4-7, 1942 — almost exactly six months after Pearl Harbor — is the direct military consequence and corrective. The Japanese plan was to force the American Pacific Fleet into a decisive battle by threatening Midway Island. American code breakers decoded the Midway operation in detail: Admiral Nimitz knew the Japanese force composition, approach routes, and approximate timing. American dive bombers, guided by a serendipitous sighting after hours of failed attacks, found the Japanese carriers at the one moment when their decks were loaded with fueled and armed aircraft. In five minutes, three Japanese fleet carriers were mortally hit. A fourth was sunk that afternoon. Japan lost four fleet carriers, 248 aircraft, and over three thousand men — including the elite carrier pilots who had executed the Pearl Harbor raid. Midway did not end the Pacific War. It ensured Japan could not win it.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. The connection: Pearl Harbor leaving the American carriers intact. Midway June 4, 1942 — those carriers fighting back. The code breakers: Nimitz reading the Japanese operation order in advance. The setup. The battle: American dive bombers finding the Japanese carriers at the one moment their decks are loaded. The five minutes: three carriers hit simultaneously. The explosions and fires. The fourth carrier that afternoon. Japan loses four carriers and its best carrier pilots. The strategic effect: Japan can never again project carrier power offensively. The Pearl Harbor pilots who executed the raid, most of them dying at Midway. The six-month reckoning. 16:9.

scenes.title: Japanese-American Internment — America's Darkest Response
scenes.narration: Pearl Harbor produced, alongside military mobilization, one of the most serious violations of civil liberties in American history. Executive Order 9066, signed by Roosevelt on February 19, 1942, authorized the removal of over 120,000 Japanese Americans from the West Coast to inland internment camps. Two-thirds were American citizens. They had 48 hours to dispose of their property. The stated justification was military necessity. No evidence of organized Japanese-American disloyalty was ever produced. Despite internment, Japanese-American men volunteered for military service in such numbers that the 442nd Infantry Regiment became the most decorated unit in American military history for its size and length of service. The Supreme Court upheld the internment in Korematsu v. United States in 1944. In 1988, Congress formally apologized and paid reparations to surviving internees.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Executive Order 9066: Roosevelt signing it, February 1942. Japanese-American families receiving relocation orders: 48 hours, abandon everything. The internment camps in desert and mountain locations: barbed wire, guard towers, barracks. American citizens behind wire. The 442nd Infantry Regiment in Europe: Japanese-American soldiers fighting for a country that imprisoned their families, becoming the most decorated unit of their size in American military history. The Korematsu case: the Supreme Court upholding internment. The 1988 apology and reparations. Pearl Harbor as the origin of military necessity as a justification for civil rights violations. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Tora Tora Tora Signal — The Attack's Precise Execution
scenes.narration: The attack on Pearl Harbor was, from a military-technical standpoint, a masterpiece of planning and execution. The signal 'Tora Tora Tora' — transmitted by flight commander Mitsuo Fuchida when he confirmed surprise had been achieved — marked the beginning of one of the most precisely executed military operations of the 20th century. The planning had taken months: the altitude of torpedo runs calibrated for Pearl Harbor's shallow harbor using technology developed from studying the British attack on Taranto, the formation assignments for the two waves, the timing to arrive at dawn. Of the planned objectives, most were achieved: five battleships sunk or severely damaged, 188 aircraft destroyed. The torpedo planes came in so low that pilots could see the expressions on American sailors' faces. Military excellence in the service of a strategic catastrophe.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. The Japanese planning: training on Kagoshima Bay, shaped like Pearl Harbor. The torpedo modification: shallow-running fins for Pearl Harbor's 40-foot depth. Fuchida in his aircraft, the fleet visible below. 'Tora Tora Tora' transmitted at 7:53 AM. The two waves in tactical detail: torpedo planes coming in low, dive bombers from altitude, fighters suppressing air response. The results: the battleship scoreboard. The technical precision of the execution compared to the strategic error of the decision. Military professionalism and strategic miscalculation in the same operation. 16:9.

scenes.title: FDR's Day of Infamy Address — The Power of Language in Crisis
scenes.narration: Franklin Roosevelt's address to Congress on December 8, 1941 lasted seven minutes and thirty seconds. The original draft, written by Roosevelt himself, began: 'a date which will live in world history, a surprise attack was made...' Roosevelt changed 'world history' to 'infamy' — a single word choice that transformed the sentence. 'Infamy' implies moral condemnation where 'history' is neutral. The phrase 'day of infamy' defined the Japanese attack not as a military event but as a moral crime. The rest of the speech listed the multiple simultaneous Japanese attacks — Malaya, Hong Kong, Guam, the Philippines, Wake Island — making clear Pearl Harbor was the opening of a coordinated assault. The speech produced a declaration of war that passed 82-0 in the Senate and 388-1 in the House. 'Day of infamy' is still how December 7 is described eighty years later.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Roosevelt at his desk, December 8, 1941: the original draft in his handwriting. The crossing out of 'world history' and insertion of 'infamy' — the manuscript close-up. The speech delivered to Congress: the packed chamber, the radio microphones. The word 'infamy' broadcast across America. The multiple theaters listed. The vote: 82-0 in the Senate, 388-1 in the House. The one dissent: Jeannette Rankin of Montana. The power of a single word choice: 'infamy' vs. 'history.' Language that shaped memory for eighty years. 16:9.

scenes.title: Hitler's Declaration of War — The Worst Strategic Decision of WWII
scenes.narration: Pearl Harbor created a problem for Germany that Hitler solved in the worst possible way. Japan was a German ally, and Pearl Harbor brought the United States into the Pacific War. The United States was not yet at war with Germany. Hitler declared war on the United States on December 11, 1941 — four days after Pearl Harbor. His motivation was partly solidarity with Japan, partly belief that the U-boat campaign could strangle American supply lines before American industrial power could mobilize, and partly conviction that America was already an undeclared enemy. He was right about the last point. But by declaring war, he gave Roosevelt the formal casus belli the isolationist resistance had prevented him from manufacturing. The America First movement collapsed overnight. Hitler's declaration of war on the United States may be the single worst strategic decision of World War II.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. December 11, 1941: Hitler at the Reichstag, declaring war on the United States. The strategic calculation: U-boat campaign, American already an enemy, solidarity with Japan. FDR receiving the German declaration: the political constraint of America First dissolving overnight. The two-front American war: Pacific against Japan, Atlantic and European against Germany. Hitler's strategic error: the one power that could tip the balance in Europe made a formal enemy before it could be isolated. The America First movement silenced by both Pearl Harbor and the German declaration. The decision that sealed Germany's fate — not made by an Allied general, but by Hitler himself. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Pacific War Context — Larger Conflict Behind Pearl Harbor
scenes.narration: Pearl Harbor occurred within a larger context of Japanese military conduct in Asia that the American public in December 1941 was only partially aware of. The Rape of Nanking in 1937 — in which Japanese forces killed between 40,000 and 300,000 Chinese civilians — had been reported in the American press but had not produced American intervention. The American oil embargo imposed in 1941 was largely a response to Japan's war in China. The Pacific War that Pearl Harbor triggered was thus the final act of a larger conflict: Japan's attempt to build a Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere — an empire throughout Asia — that had been proceeding for a decade before the United States became directly involved. The context does not diminish the magnitude of the Pearl Harbor attack. It explains why the Pacific War was fought with such ferocity on both sides.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. The context before Pearl Harbor: Japanese expansion in Asia beginning 1931. The Manchurian Incident. The full-scale China war from 1937. The Rape of Nanking. American press coverage and non-intervention. The Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere: Japan's imperial vision on a map. The American oil embargo. The chain: embargo, Japan's fuel crisis, the need to take the Dutch East Indies, the need to neutralize the American Pacific fleet, Pearl Harbor. The war Pearl Harbor started as the culmination of a decade of Asian conflict. The atrocities that followed on both sides: Bataan Death March, Tokyo firebombing, Hiroshima, Nagasaki. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Arizona Memorial — America's Most Visited War Memorial
scenes.narration: The USS Arizona Memorial, built over the sunken hull in 1962, receives more than two million visitors annually. The design by Alfred Preis — an Austrian-American architect who was himself interned after Pearl Harbor — is deliberately ambiguous: the structure sags in the middle and rises at both ends, representing defeat followed by final victory. Visitors arrive by boat — you cannot walk to the memorial, you can only be ferried to it. Below the structure, through the viewing area in the floor, you can see the rusting hull and, when the water is calm, the oil still rising from her tanks. The memorial is not technically on the ship. It is above it. The men below are still on their ship. The living who visit are always above; the dead remain below, on their ship, in their harbor.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. The Arizona Memorial: the white structure over the harbor, the ferry approaching it. Alfred Preis's design: the deliberate sag and rise — defeat, then victory. The transition: arriving by boat, the pause before entering. The interior: the white marble wall with the names. The viewing area: the harbor water below, the rusting hull visible. The oil rising — the 'black tears' on the surface. The geometry of the memorial: visitors above, the ship below. The 900 men who remain on their ship. Two million people per year making the ferry crossing. 16:9.

scenes.title: Pearl Harbor's 80-Year Memory — How the Attack Became a National Template
scenes.narration: Pearl Harbor entered American memory in a way that very few events have: it became a moral template. The concept of the surprise attack — the unprovoked strike against a peaceful nation at rest — became one of the central organizing principles of American national security thinking. The lesson drawn — never be caught unprepared again — drove the creation of the CIA, the NSA, and the vast American intelligence apparatus. The phrase 'another Pearl Harbor' entered political language as shorthand for the worst kind of intelligence failure. When September 11, 2001 occurred, the immediate comparison was to Pearl Harbor. The 9/11 Commission report explicitly invoked Pearl Harbor as a failure model. The attack of December 7, 1941 is still the frame through which Americans conceptualize surprise and vulnerability. The event that happened once became the lens through which subsequent events are seen forever.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Pearl Harbor's afterlife in American culture and policy: the CIA founded in 1947, partly in response to Pearl Harbor's intelligence failure. The NSA. The Pacific alliances. The phrase 'another Pearl Harbor' in political speeches across decades. September 11, 2001: the immediate Pearl Harbor comparison in press and political coverage. The 9/11 Commission report citing Pearl Harbor as the intelligence failure template. The memorial ceremonies: December 7 each year, the survivors aging and disappearing. The last Pearl Harbor survivor alive. What it means for one morning's attack to shape a nation's security psychology for eighty years and beyond. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Kido Butai — Six Carriers That Changed Naval Warfare
scenes.narration: The force that attacked Pearl Harbor — the Kido Butai, or 'mobile force' — represented the most powerful concentration of naval air power ever assembled to that point. Six fleet carriers: Akagi, Kaga, Soryu, Hiryu, Shokaku, and Zuikaku, carrying 414 aircraft, with a supporting force of battleships, cruisers, and destroyers. The assembly of six carriers in a single strike force was a radical departure from the battleship-centered naval doctrine of every major navy in the world. The Japanese naval aviation community had spent a decade developing the doctrine, the aircraft, and the tactics to make carrier strike forces the primary offensive naval weapon. Pearl Harbor proved their theory correct — and simultaneously demonstrated it to every other navy in the world. The battleship era ended not at Pearl Harbor but in the moment the Kido Butai struck it.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. The Kido Butai: the six fleet carriers shown — Akagi, Kaga, Soryu, Hiryu, Shokaku, Zuikaku. The formation at sea: six carriers, their escorts, 414 aircraft below decks. The departure from Hitokappu Bay on November 26, sailing north to avoid detection. The six carriers launching in the pre-dawn darkness on December 7. The demonstration of carrier warfare doctrine: what six carriers can do to a battleship fleet. Every naval officer watching Pearl Harbor understanding simultaneously that the battleship era is over. The most consequential demonstration of a new military technology since the cannon made the medieval castle obsolete. 16:9.

scenes.title: Yamamoto's Biography — The Admiral Who Predicted His Own Country's Defeat
scenes.narration: Isoroku Yamamoto, the architect of the Pearl Harbor attack, is one of the most tragically complex figures of World War II. He had lived in the United States, studied at Harvard, and understood American industrial capacity in a way that most Japanese military leaders did not. He opposed the alliance with Germany and opposed the decision for war with the United States. He reportedly told the prime minister: 'I can run wild for six months or a year, but I have utterly no confidence for the second or third year of the fighting.' He was then ordered to plan the war he predicted would fail. He planned it with brilliant precision. He was killed on April 18, 1943, when American code breakers identified his inspection tour of the Solomon Islands and P-38 fighters shot down his transport plane over Bougainville. The admiral who predicted his country's defeat was shot down by the country whose industrial power he had warned about.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Yamamoto's biography: his time in the United States, studying at Harvard, understanding American industry. His opposition to the war: his warning to the prime minister. His face when ordered to plan the attack he predicted would fail. The brilliance of the Pearl Harbor plan: the training, the torpedo modifications, the surprise achieved. Then: April 18, 1943. American code breakers reading his inspection schedule. P-38 fighters over Bougainville. His transport plane shot down. The admiral who warned against fighting America, killed by the code breakers whose capability he understood better than his own government did. The man who predicted everything that happened to him. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Oil Tanks That Weren't Hit — Why Pearl Harbor Wasn't Total
scenes.narration: The Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor achieved most of its planned objectives but left undone one target that, in retrospect, was more important than any of the battleships it sank: the oil storage tanks at Pearl Harbor. The tanks held approximately 4.5 million barrels of oil — essentially the entire fuel reserve for the Pacific Fleet. The Japanese commanders decided not to strike the oil tanks because their strike plan focused on the ships. Rear Admiral Husband Kimmel, the Pacific Fleet commander, acknowledged after the war that had the oil tanks been destroyed, the Pacific Fleet would have had to withdraw to the West Coast, and the war in the Pacific would have been set back by two years minimum. The Japanese hit what they could see — the ships — and missed the infrastructure that sustained the ships. The attack that appeared total was, in one critical respect, not total. The oil that fueled the carriers at Midway was untouched.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. The Pearl Harbor oil storage tanks: the cylindrical tanks visible on the hillside above the harbor, full of 4.5 million barrels of fuel. The Japanese strike plan: the battleships, the airfields, the ships in the harbor. The tanks not on the primary target list. The decision not to strike them. Kimmel's postwar testimony: if the tanks had gone, the Pacific Fleet retreats to the West Coast, the war in the Pacific delayed by two years. The tanks still standing after the attack, still full. The fuel that would power the carriers at Midway six months later, untouched. The attack that seemed devastating, and the one target that would have made it complete, left undone. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Undeclared War — What Niihau Island Reveals About Pearl Harbor's Complexity
scenes.narration: The story of Niihau Island, seventeen miles west of Kauai, is one of the strangest footnotes of Pearl Harbor. On December 7, Japanese pilot Shigenori Nishikaichi, his Zero damaged during the attack, made an emergency landing on Niihau — a privately owned island with no radio contact with the outside world. The island's small population didn't initially know about Pearl Harbor. Several Japanese-American residents of the island helped the downed pilot, destroying papers, taking residents captive. The incident ended with the pilot dead after a struggle. The story reveals multiple complexities: the vulnerability of American territory even after the attack, the position of Japanese Americans caught between competing loyalties, and the genuine ambiguity that existed in the first hours after Pearl Harbor about what exactly was happening and what it meant. The War Department used the Niihau incident as partial justification for the internment order. One plane on one island made its way into one of the biggest policy decisions of the war.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Niihau Island: the remote, privately owned island seventeen miles west of Kauai. The damaged Zero landing. Pilot Nishikaichi and the island's population, who don't know Pearl Harbor has happened. The Japanese-American residents caught between competing loyalties. The incident: the papers destroyed, residents taken captive, the ending with the pilot dead. The War Department's use of the Niihau incident as justification for internment. One small island, one downed pilot, one footnote that reveals the genuine complexity of the first hours after Pearl Harbor. What happens when national loyalties and human realities collide in real time. 16:9.

scenes.title: The War That Pearl Harbor Made — America's Century Begins
scenes.narration: World War II — specifically the American victory in it — created the world we live in. The United Nations, the International Monetary Fund, the World Bank, NATO, the modern system of international law — all emerged from the post-war settlement that American power shaped. The dollar became the world's reserve currency at Bretton Woods in 1944. The American military presence in Europe and the Pacific, established during the war, never fully withdrew: American bases in Japan and Germany are still operating. The postwar American economy, supercharged by wartime production and the only major industrial economy not destroyed by the war, generated prosperity that redefined the middle class. Pearl Harbor, by forcing American entry into the war, was thus the beginning of the American century — the era of American global dominance that shaped the second half of the 20th century. The attack that was meant to keep America out of the Pacific, set the conditions for America to dominate it.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Pearl Harbor to the postwar world: American entry December 1941. American factories converting to war production. The scale of American industrial mobilization: 300,000 aircraft built, 86,000 tanks, 8.5 million soldiers armed. The postwar settlement shaped by American power: the UN, IMF, World Bank, NATO. Bretton Woods 1944: the dollar as world reserve currency. American bases in Japan and Germany today — still there, 80 years later. The postwar American economy: the only major economy not destroyed. The paradox: the attack meant to keep America out of the Pacific set the conditions for America to dominate the world. The Japanese fleet that sailed to Pearl Harbor on November 26, 1941, sailing into the American century. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Attack's Three Waves — What Would Have Happened if Nagumo Had Launched a Third
scenes.narration: After the second wave of Japanese aircraft departed Pearl Harbor, Vice Admiral Chuichi Nagumo — commander of the Kido Butai — made the decision to withdraw without launching a third strike. The debate about this decision has continued since 1941. A third strike, targeting the oil storage tanks and repair facilities, could have extended the Pacific War by two or more years. The arguments for withdrawal were not unreasonable: the element of surprise was gone, the location of the American aircraft carriers was unknown, the Kido Butai's aircraft had taken losses, and an extended operation in enemy waters increased risk to his fleet. The arguments against: a third strike on the oil and dry docks would have been decisive in a way the first two were not. Nagumo chose conservative military logic over strategic opportunity. It was professionally defensible. It may have cost Japan the war in the Pacific.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Nagumo on the bridge of the Akagi: the second wave returning, the aircraft landing on the carriers. His staff presenting arguments for and against a third strike. The oil tanks visible from reconnaissance aircraft: 4.5 million barrels of fuel. The dry docks. Nagumo's face: the conservative military commander's calculation of risk vs. reward. The Kido Butai turning northwest to withdraw. The oil tanks standing intact as the fleet sails away. The counterfactual: the map showing what happens if the oil goes — the Pacific Fleet retreating to the West Coast, the two-year delay. The decision not made, and what it cost. 16:9.

scenes.title: December 7 in American Memory — The Date That Defines a Generation
scenes.narration: December 7, 1941 became for the World War II generation what November 22, 1963 became for the Kennedy generation and September 11, 2001 became for the post-Cold War generation: the date that defines before and after. Americans who were alive on that Sunday — across every part of the country, whatever they were doing — remember where they were when they heard the news. The radio announcements, the newspaper extras, the gathering around radios in living rooms and bars and churches. The cultural processing of December 7 has continued for eight decades: the films, the memorials, the annual ceremonies at Pearl Harbor, the interviews with surviving veterans that have now ended as the last survivors have died. The generation that fought and won World War II is gone. The memory of December 7 is now historical rather than lived. But the date — and the phrase 'day of infamy' — remains the fixed star of American 20th century memory, the point from which everything else in the wartime decade is measured.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. December 7, 1941 in American memory: families gathering around radios in living rooms, the radio announcements breaking in on Sunday afternoon programming. Newspaper extras being printed and thrown from trucks. Young men in bars and churches hearing the news and deciding, then and there, to enlist. The cultural processing over decades: the films, the ceremonies at Pearl Harbor each December 7. Veterans in old age at the memorial: the last survivors. The transition from lived memory to historical memory. The phrase 'day of infamy' remaining in the language eight decades later. The date that divided American life into before and after, still dividing it. 16:9.

scenes.title: Next Episode — D-Day
scenes.narration: Next episode on Gods and Glory: three years after Pearl Harbor, the United States and its allies launched the largest amphibious invasion in human history onto the beaches of Normandy. Five beaches. Thousands of ships. One single day that decided whether Nazi-occupied Europe would ever be free. D-Day — how the Allies pulled off the impossible, and what it actually cost.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic historical documentary. Teaser shot: a vast Allied invasion fleet approaching a coastline at dawn, landing craft full of soldiers, the scale of D-Day suggested. Epic, anticipatory. 16:9.
